stem: A 38-year-old man with 55% total body surface area flame burns is in the ICU 18 hours after injury, having received aggressive crystalloid resuscitation (well above his calculated Parkland volume because of poor early urine output). He is intubated and sedated. Over the past 2 hours his peak inspiratory pressures have climbed from 28 to 44 cm H2O on volume-control ventilation, his urine output has fallen to 8 mL/hr, and his mean arterial pressure has dropped to 58 mmHg despite escalating norepinephrine. The abdomen is tensely distended. A bladder (intravesical) pressure transduced at end-expiration reads 26 mmHg. Which intervention most directly addresses the underlying pathophysiology of this deterioration?

choices.text: Increase the norepinephrine infusion and add vasopressin to restore mean arterial pressure
choices.rationale: Vasopressors treat the downstream hypotension but do nothing to relieve the elevated intra-abdominal pressure that is compressing the IVC and reducing venous return.

choices.text: Administer additional crystalloid boluses to improve the falling urine output
choices.rationale: More volume worsens visceral and abdominal wall edema, raising intra-abdominal pressure further and aggravating the secondary abdominal compartment syndrome already driven by over-resuscitation.

choices.text: Increase PEEP and tidal volume to overcome the rising peak inspiratory pressures
choices.rationale: The high airway pressures reflect cephalad diaphragmatic displacement from the distended abdomen, and adding PEEP further impedes venous return without lowering intra-abdominal pressure.

choices.text: Perform decompressive laparotomy to relieve the intra-abdominal hypertension
choices.rationale: Sustained intra-abdominal pressure greater than 20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction (oliguria, hypotension, rising airway pressures) defines abdominal compartment syndrome, and surgical decompression is the definitive treatment when it is refractory.

choices.text: Start renal-dose dopamine and a furosemide infusion to reverse the oliguria
choices.rationale: The oliguria is from renal vein and parenchymal compression plus reduced cardiac output, not a primary renal problem, so diuretics and low-dose dopamine will not restore perfusion.

explanation: Abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS) is defined as a sustained intra-abdominal pressure (IAP) >20 mmHg accompanied by new-onset organ dysfunction. In burns, massive crystalloid resuscitation causes bowel wall and mesenteric edema, producing secondary ACS even without abdominal trauma. The rising IAP compresses the IVC and renal vessels (decreasing preload, cardiac output, and urine output) and displaces the diaphragm cephalad (raising peak airway pressures). When the syndrome is established and refractory to medical measures, decompressive laparotomy is the definitive intervention; ongoing fluid loading and pressors only treat or worsen the consequences.
subtopic: Secondary abdominal compartment syndrome after burn over-resuscitation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The vignette is textbook secondary abdominal compartment syndrome from burn over-resuscitation: IAP 26 mmHg (Grade IV, >20) measured correctly by bladder pressure at end-expiration, plus new multi-organ dysfunction (oliguria 8 mL/hr, hypotension/MAP 58 on escalating norepinephrine, rising PIP 28->44 from cephalad diaphragm displacement). This meets the WSACS definition of ACS (sustained IAP >20 mmHg + new organ dysfunction). The keyed answer (index 3, decompressive laparotomy) is the single best option: the patient is already refractory to escalating pressors and deteriorating, and laparotomy is the only choice that addresses the underlying intra-abdominal hypertension. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: more pressors treat only the downstream hypotension; more crystalloid worsens visceral/abdominal-wall edema and raises IAP; increasing PEEP/TV impedes venous return without lowering IAP; renal-dose dopamine + furosemide is correctly disparaged (the oliguria is from renal vein/parenchymal compression and low cardiac output, not a primary renal problem, and renal-dose dopamine is an abandoned, evidence-free intervention). Although WSACS endorses a stepwise medical algorithm (sedation, NMB, NG/rectal decompression, percutaneous drainage) before surgery, none of those less-invasive measures appear in the option set, so there is no competing defensible answer; the stem explicitly frames the syndrome as established and refractory. The explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430932/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Abdominal Compartment Syndrome' chapter, which directly covers the IAP >20 mmHg + organ dysfunction definition, bladder-catheter measurement as gold standard, secondary ACS from massive crystalloid/fluid resuscitation, and decompressive laparotomy as definitive treatment when conservative management fails. Citation is on-topic and supports the keyed answer. Both checks pass; keep.

stem: A 24-year-old previously healthy man sustains a closed midshaft femur fracture and a closed tibial fracture in a motorcycle crash. He is hemodynamically stable on admission, with definitive intramedullary nailing planned for the following morning. Roughly 36 hours after the injury, while still awaiting the OR, he becomes acutely confused and agitated, his SpO2 falls to 86% on room air, and the nurse notes a scattered petechial rash across his chest, axillae, and conjunctivae. His temperature is 38.4°C and heart rate is 118/min. A chest radiograph shows bilateral diffuse infiltrates and the head CT is unremarkable. Which mechanism best explains this constellation of findings?

choices.text: Acute pulmonary thromboembolism from a lower-extremity deep vein thrombosis
choices.rationale: A bland venous thromboembolus causes hypoxemia and tachycardia but does not produce the characteristic non-dependent petechiae or the prominent neurologic changes, and it would not typically present with diffuse bilateral infiltrates and fever.

choices.text: Fat embolism syndrome from marrow fat and inflammatory mediators released by the long-bone fractures
choices.rationale: The classic triad of hypoxemia, neurologic dysfunction, and a petechial rash appearing 24-72 hours after long-bone fractures is the hallmark of fat embolism syndrome, driven by both mechanical embolization of marrow fat and a biochemical inflammatory cascade.

choices.text: Air embolism introduced through an open fracture site during transfers
choices.rationale: Venous air embolism causes sudden cardiopulmonary collapse at the moment of air entrainment, not a delayed 36-hour onset with petechiae, and these were closed fractures.

choices.text: Early ventilator-associated pneumonia with septic showering
choices.rationale: This patient was not described as ventilated before the event, and pneumonia does not explain the conjunctival and axillary petechiae or the acute encephalopathy.

choices.text: Transfusion-related acute lung injury from blood products given during resuscitation
choices.rationale: TRALI produces hypoxemia and bilateral infiltrates within 6 hours of transfusion, but it does not cause petechiae and is tied temporally to a recent product, not a 36-hour post-fracture window.

explanation: Fat embolism syndrome (FES) classically presents 24-72 hours after long-bone or pelvic fractures with the triad of hypoxemia (respiratory distress), neurologic abnormalities (confusion, agitation), and a petechial rash that favors non-dependent areas such as the conjunctivae, axillae, and chest. The pathophysiology is both mechanical (marrow fat embolizing to the pulmonary and systemic circulation) and biochemical (free fatty acids producing a toxic inflammatory pneumonitis). Diagnosis is clinical (Gurd/Wilson criteria), and management is largely supportive with oxygenation and early fracture fixation; the delayed onset and petechiae distinguish it from thromboembolism, air embolism, and TRALI.
subtopic: Fat embolism syndrome in long-bone trauma

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (Fat embolism syndrome, index 1) is unambiguously the single best answer. The vignette is a textbook FES presentation: young man with closed femur + tibia fractures, onset ~36h post-injury (within the classic 24-72h window), the full triad of hypoxemia, neurologic dysfunction (confusion/agitation), and petechial rash in characteristic non-dependent areas (conjunctivae, axillae, chest), plus tachycardia, low-grade fever, and bilateral diffuse infiltrates. Normal head CT is consistent with FES (cerebral involvement often subtle on CT). All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly refuted: PTE/bland thrombus does not cause non-dependent petechiae or prominent encephalopathy; air embolism causes immediate collapse at entrainment and these were closed fractures; VAP is excluded because the patient was not ventilated and pneumonia does not explain petechiae/encephalopathy; TRALI occurs <6h after transfusion and causes no petechiae (and no transfusion is even described, making it more clearly wrong, not ambiguous). The explanation's pathophysiology (dual mechanical + biochemical), timing, petechial distribution, clinical Gurd/Wilson diagnosis, and supportive management are all accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499885/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Fat Embolism' article and is directly on-topic, explicitly confirming the 24-72h onset window, the triad including petechial rash as a major criterion, both mechanical and biochemical theories, and Gurd/Wilson criteria as the standard clinical diagnosis. No reference problem.

stem: A 29-year-old electrical lineworker is brought to the emergency department after contact with a high-voltage line. He has a small entry wound on the right palm and an exit wound on the left foot, but the visible skin burns appear minor. He is awake and complaining of severe muscle pain. His vital signs are stable, but the cardiac monitor shows frequent multifocal premature ventricular contractions and the urine in the catheter bag is dark reddish-brown. A dipstick is strongly positive for blood, yet microscopy shows essentially no red blood cells. Which statement best guides the immediate management priorities for this injury pattern?

choices.text: The minor-appearing skin wounds reliably reflect the true extent of injury, so observation alone is appropriate
choices.rationale: High-voltage current causes extensive deep tissue and muscle necrosis along its path that is grossly underestimated by the small cutaneous entry and exit wounds.

choices.text: The dark urine indicates hemoglobinuria from hemolysis, so the priority is type-and-cross for transfusion
choices.rationale: A dipstick positive for blood with no red cells on microscopy in this setting reflects myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis, not hemoglobin from hemolysis.

choices.text: Cardiac monitoring is unnecessary because the patient is hemodynamically stable on arrival
choices.rationale: Electrical injury can precipitate dysrhythmias and delayed arrhythmic events, and the multifocal PVCs already present mandate continuous telemetry rather than discharge.

choices.text: Fluid restriction should be used to prevent pulmonary edema given the deep burns
choices.rationale: Restricting fluids in the face of rhabdomyolysis promotes pigment precipitation and acute kidney injury, the opposite of the aggressive hydration these patients require.

choices.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain brisk urine output is needed because rhabdomyolysis is driving the pigmented urine and threatens the kidneys
choices.rationale: Myoglobin released from current-induced muscle necrosis causes pigment-induced acute kidney injury, and early generous fluid administration targeting a high urine output is the cornerstone of preventing it.

explanation: High-voltage electrical injury produces deep tissue and skeletal muscle necrosis far exceeding the small cutaneous entry/exit wounds, releasing myoglobin that causes pigment-induced acute kidney injury. A urine dipstick positive for blood with no red cells on microscopy is the classic clue to myoglobinuria rather than hemoglobinuria. Management centers on aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain a high urine output and flush the pigment, alongside continuous cardiac monitoring because electrical current can trigger dysrhythmias. Fluid restriction and reliance on the deceptively minor skin burns are dangerous in this injury pattern.
subtopic: High-voltage electrical injury — rhabdomyolysis, pigmenturia, and dysrhythmia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 4 — aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain brisk urine output because rhabdomyolysis/myoglobin threatens the kidneys) is the single best answer and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook high-voltage electrical injury: small cutaneous entry/exit wounds masking deep muscle necrosis, severe muscle pain, multifocal PVCs, and dark reddish-brown urine that is dipstick-positive for blood with no RBCs on microscopy (classic myoglobinuria, not hemoglobinuria). All four distractors are genuinely inferior and the rationales are accurate: (0) cutaneous wounds grossly underestimate deep injury — false; (1) dipstick+ with no RBCs reflects myoglobin from rhabdomyolysis, not hemolysis, so type-and-cross is wrong; (2) the patient already has multifocal PVCs, so continuous telemetry is mandated, not discharge; (3) fluid restriction worsens pigment-induced AKI. Aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation targeting high urine output is the established cornerstone of preventing pigment nephropathy, and because this patient's cutaneous burns are minimal there is no tension with the caveat against diuretics/alkalinization in large (>20% TBSA) cutaneous burns. Content is correct and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448087/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Electrical Injuries' chapter, which directly covers rhabdomyolysis, myoglobinuria, AKI risk, fluid resuscitation (target urine output 1-2 mL/kg/h), and dysrhythmias — fully on-topic. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 64-year-old man (BMI 24, 40-pack-year smoking history) is undergoing a right upper lobectomy via thoracotomy in the left lateral decubitus position. A left-sided double-lumen tube is in good position, confirmed by fiberoptic bronchoscopy, and one-lung ventilation of the dependent left lung has been initiated with the non-dependent right lung open to air. Twenty minutes later, with FiO2 1.0, tidal volume 6 mL/kg, and PEEP 5 cmH2O to the ventilated lung, the SpO2 drifts from 98% to 86%. Breath sounds are present on the dependent side, peak pressures are unchanged, and the surgeon has not yet ligated any pulmonary vessels. Which of the following is the most appropriate NEXT step to improve oxygenation?

choices.text: Immediately abandon one-lung ventilation and resume two-lung ventilation
choices.rationale: Resuming two-lung ventilation is the definitive rescue but is reserved for refractory or profound desaturation; it is premature before simpler maneuvers and would interrupt the surgeon's exposure.

choices.text: Increase the tidal volume to the dependent lung to 12 mL/kg to recruit atelectatic alveoli
choices.rationale: Large tidal volumes overdistend the dependent lung, raise its vascular resistance, and divert blood toward the collapsed lung, worsening shunt and risking acute lung injury.

choices.text: Reconfirm tube position with the bronchoscope, then apply CPAP to the non-dependent (operative) lung
choices.rationale: Excluding tube malposition first and then applying CPAP to the non-ventilated lung delivers oxygen to its perfused-but-collapsed alveoli, directly reducing transpulmonary shunt while preserving surgical access.

choices.text: Discontinue the PEEP applied to the dependent lung
choices.rationale: Removing PEEP from the dependent lung promotes its atelectasis and reduces functional residual capacity, which typically worsens rather than improves oxygenation during one-lung ventilation.

choices.text: Administer a phenylephrine infusion to raise systemic vascular resistance and improve perfusion pressure
choices.rationale: Systemic vasopressors do not correct intrapulmonary shunt and may blunt hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, so they will not meaningfully reverse this hypoxemia.

explanation: Hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation is driven by transpulmonary shunt through the collapsed, non-ventilated lung. After ruling out a mechanical cause, the first-line non-surgical maneuver after confirming FiO2 1.0 is to reconfirm double-lumen tube position with the bronchoscope (malposition is the most common correctable cause), then apply CPAP (typically 5-10 cmH2O) to the operative lung to oxygenate its perfused alveoli, often combined with PEEP and recruitment of the dependent lung. Returning to two-lung ventilation is the rescue of last resort for refractory or severe desaturation, and the surgeon can also clamp the pulmonary artery to eliminate shunt once vessels are accessible.
subtopic: One-lung ventilation — management of hypoxemia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Scenario is a gradual, moderate desaturation (98%->86%) during OLV with FiO2 already 1.0 and no surgical vessel ligation yet. The keyed answer (C: reconfirm DLT position with bronchoscope, then apply CPAP to the non-dependent/operative lung) reflects the standard algorithm: rule out malposition (the most common correctable cause, and DLTs migrate with lateral positioning/surgical manipulation even after initial confirmation), then apply CPAP to the perfused-but-collapsed operative lung to reduce transpulmonary shunt. The reassuring exam findings (breath sounds present, stable peak pressures) do not undermine re-checking position, which remains the recommended first verification step. All distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: A (return to two-lung ventilation) is the rescue of last resort for severe/refractory hypoxemia and is premature; B (TV 12 mL/kg) causes dependent-lung overdistension, raises PVR, diverts flow to the shunting lung, and risks lung injury; D (remove PEEP) worsens FRC/atelectasis; E (phenylephrine) does not correct intrapulmonary shunt and may blunt HPV. No competing 'recruit dependent lung' option exists among the choices, so C is the single best answer with no ambiguity. The explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538314/ resolves (HTTP 200) and is the StatPearls 'Single-Lung Ventilation' chapter, directly on-topic, explicitly stating 'CPAP to the operative lung may be applied to decrease shunting and thus improve oxygenation.' The page is flagged '(Archived)' but loads and is fully relevant — a minor cosmetic note, not a broken or mismatched citation. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension, a remote drug-eluting coronary stent placed 4 years ago (asymptomatic, on aspirin), and an ejection fraction of 55% is undergoing open repair of a 6-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm under general endotracheal anesthesia with an arterial line and central venous catheter. As the surgeon applies the infrarenal aortic cross-clamp, you anticipate the hemodynamic consequences. Which of the following changes is MOST likely to occur immediately after this infrarenal clamp is applied?

choices.text: A marked, reliable increase in cardiac preload as blood is displaced from the clamped aortic segment into the central circulation
choices.rationale: A large, reliable preload rise occurs with a supraceliac clamp; with an infrarenal clamp the splanchnic venous bed accommodates the displaced volume, so preload does not predictably increase.

choices.text: An increase in afterload (systemic vascular resistance) and arterial blood pressure proximal to the clamp
choices.rationale: Occluding the aorta abruptly raises impedance to left ventricular ejection, increasing proximal arterial pressure and afterload, which is the dominant and expected response to clamping at any level.

choices.text: A fall in systemic vascular resistance due to reflex vasodilation distal to the clamp
choices.rationale: Systemic vascular resistance rises rather than falls with clamping; distal hypoperfusion does not produce a net reflex drop in measured systemic resistance proximally.

choices.text: A profound, sustained drop in arterial blood pressure requiring immediate vasopressor support
choices.rationale: Hypotension is characteristic of clamp RELEASE (reactive hyperemia and washout of vasoactive metabolites), not of clamp application, which instead causes hypertension proximally.

choices.text: A reduction in left ventricular wall tension and myocardial oxygen demand
choices.rationale: The increased afterload raises left ventricular wall tension and myocardial oxygen demand, so demand increases rather than decreases, a key concern in this patient with coronary disease.

explanation: Aortic cross-clamping abruptly increases impedance to left ventricular ejection, raising afterload and proximal arterial pressure regardless of clamp level. The magnitude of the preload change, however, is level-dependent: a supraceliac clamp redistributes splanchnic venous blood centrally and reliably raises preload, whereas an infrarenal clamp is accommodated by the compliant splanchnic venous bed so preload typically does not rise. The increased afterload elevates left ventricular wall tension and myocardial oxygen demand, which is especially relevant in a patient with known coronary disease, and unclamping classically produces hypotension from reactive hyperemia and metabolite washout.
subtopic: Open AAA repair — aortic cross-clamp hemodynamics

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (B/index 1 — increased afterload/SVR and proximal arterial pressure) is the single best answer and reflects the well-established Gelman physiology of aortic cross-clamping: increased impedance to LV ejection and proximal hypertension occur at ANY clamp level, making it the dominant, level-independent response. All distractors are genuinely inferior and the rationales are accurate: (A) a reliable preload rise is characteristic of a SUPRACELIAC clamp, whereas an infrarenal clamp is buffered by the compliant splanchnic venous bed so preload does not predictably rise — correctly distinguished and level-dependent; (C) SVR rises rather than falls; (D) profound sustained hypotension is the UNCLAMPING response (reactive hyperemia/metabolite washout), not clamp application; (E) wrong direction — increased afterload raises LV wall tension and myocardial O2 demand, especially relevant with this patient's coronary stent/CAD. The explanation correctly frames afterload increase as the consistent finding while preload change is level-dependent. No ambiguity; medicine is correct and current.\n\nREFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554573/ resolves (200) to the StatPearls 'Abdominal Aortic Repair' chapter, which is genuinely on-topic for open AAA repair and even notes proximal clamp placement just below the renal arteries. It is a broad surgical/EVAR overview rather than a dedicated cross-clamp hemodynamics source, but it is a real, relevant, non-fabricated citation matching the question's subtopic — acceptable.

stem: A 70-year-old woman with hypertension and an 80% symptomatic left internal carotid artery stenosis is undergoing left carotid endarterectomy under regional anesthesia (combined superficial and deep cervical plexus block) specifically so she can serve as her own awake neurologic monitor. She is conversant and moving all extremities at baseline. Shortly after the surgeon applies the carotid cross-clamp, she becomes confused, slurs her speech, and develops right-sided arm weakness. Blood pressure is 130/75 mmHg and SpO2 is 99%. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management step?

choices.text: Immediately convert to general anesthesia and secure the airway before any other intervention
choices.rationale: Converting to general anesthesia abolishes the very awake neurologic monitoring that just detected the deficit and delays the maneuver that actually restores cerebral perfusion.

choices.text: Administer a benzodiazepine, presuming the new confusion reflects local anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: A focal, lateralizing motor and speech deficit appearing exactly at clamp application reflects cerebral ischemia, not local anesthetic toxicity, and sedation would obscure ongoing neurologic assessment.

choices.text: Lower the blood pressure with a nitroglycerin infusion to reduce the risk of hemorrhagic conversion
choices.rationale: Lowering blood pressure further reduces collateral cerebral perfusion to the at-risk hemisphere and would worsen the ischemic deficit during clamping.

choices.text: Continue observation, since transient deficits at clamp application routinely resolve without intervention
choices.rationale: A new clamp-induced deficit signals inadequate collateral flow and demands prompt action; passive observation risks a completed stroke.

choices.text: Alert the surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt and support blood pressure toward the high end of her normal range
choices.rationale: Placing a shunt restores antegrade flow to the ischemic hemisphere and raising blood pressure augments collateral perfusion through the circle of Willis, directly addressing the clamp-induced ischemia.

explanation: A new contralateral neurologic deficit appearing at carotid cross-clamp application during awake carotid endarterectomy indicates inadequate collateral cerebral perfusion and is the principal indication for selective shunting. The immediate management is to alert the surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt to restore antegrade flow while augmenting blood pressure toward the high-normal range to maximize collateral flow through the circle of Willis. Lowering blood pressure or sedating the patient would worsen ischemia or eliminate the monitoring advantage of the awake technique, and conversion to general anesthesia is reserved for airway compromise, intractable agitation, or seizure rather than as the first response to an ischemic deficit.
subtopic: Carotid endarterectomy — cerebral ischemia and awake neurologic monitoring

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Left ICA cross-clamp produces left-hemisphere ischemia, which correctly manifests as contralateral (right-sided) arm weakness plus speech disturbance (dominant left hemisphere) — the stem's lateralization is medically correct. A new neurologic deficit appearing at clamp application during awake CEA is the textbook indication for selective shunting; alerting the surgeon to place an intraluminal shunt while augmenting BP to high-normal to maximize circle-of-Willis collateral flow (keyed index 4) is the single best immediate step and matches standard teaching (Miller/Barash/Stoelting). All distractors are genuinely inferior: converting to GA first abolishes monitoring and delays reperfusion (reserved for airway/agitation/seizure); a benzodiazepine for presumed LAST is wrong because a focal lateralizing hemiparesis at clamp is ischemia, not LAST, and sedation obscures monitoring; lowering BP with nitroglycerin worsens collateral perfusion; observation risks a completed stroke. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470582/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Carotid Endarterectomy' article — correct, on-topic citation that confirms the awake technique and shunting to restore flow. It is a general overview and does not detail shunting indications or cross-clamp BP management, but it is genuinely relevant and not fabricated/404/mismatched. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 68-year-old man with diabetes managed on NPH insulin and a prior history of fish allergy is undergoing on-pump coronary artery bypass grafting. After separation from cardiopulmonary bypass, the anesthesiologist asks the CAA to begin reversal of heparin. Within 60 seconds of starting the protamine infusion, the systemic arterial pressure falls from 110/65 to 55/30 mmHg, the pulmonary artery pressure rises sharply, central venous pressure climbs, and the right ventricle appears distended and poorly contractile on the surgical field. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate response?

choices.text: Stop the protamine infusion, support the circulation with vasopressors/inotropes and a pulmonary vasodilator, and consider re-heparinizing and returning to bypass
choices.rationale: This describes the classic catastrophic protamine reaction (thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction with acute RV failure); halting the offending drug and supporting the RV/pulmonary circulation, with return to bypass if needed, is the correct management.

choices.text: Continue the protamine at the same rate to ensure complete heparin neutralization, since interrupting it risks rebound heparinization
choices.rationale: Continuing the drug that is causing the reaction would worsen the pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure, so this is exactly the wrong move.

choices.text: Administer a large fluid bolus and place the patient in steep Trendelenburg as the primary intervention
choices.rationale: Aggressive volume loading a failing, distended right ventricle increases wall tension and worsens RV function rather than treating the underlying pulmonary vasoconstriction.

choices.text: Give an additional bolus of heparin to deliberately prolong the ACT and abandon any further protamine
choices.rationale: While re-heparinization may accompany a return to bypass, giving heparin alone without stopping protamine and supporting the circulation does not address the acute hemodynamic collapse.

choices.text: Treat the event as anaphylaxis to the muscle relaxant and administer high-dose epinephrine before stopping any infusions
choices.rationale: Although vasopressors/inotropes are part of management, misattributing the reaction to the relaxant and leaving the protamine running fails to remove the true trigger.

explanation: Protamine, a polycationic protein, reverses heparin (roughly 1 mg per 100 units of heparin) but can provoke a spectrum of reactions: rapid-administration hypotension (calcium/nitric-oxide mediated), anaphylactoid reactions, and a catastrophic thromboxane-A2-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction that abruptly raises pulmonary vascular resistance and precipitates acute right ventricular failure. NPH/protamine-zinc insulin use, prior protamine exposure, fish allergy, and vasectomy are recognized risk factors. Management is to stop the infusion, support the RV and pulmonary circulation with inotropes/vasopressors and pulmonary vasodilators, and be prepared to re-heparinize and resume bypass.
subtopic: Heparin reversal: protamine adverse reactions

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The vignette is a textbook Type III catastrophic protamine reaction (onset within 60s of protamine, profound systemic hypotension, sharp PA pressure rise, rising CVP, acute RV distension/failure = thromboxane-A2-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction). The keyed answer (index 0: stop protamine, support circulation with vasopressors/inotropes and a pulmonary vasodilator, consider re-heparinization and return to bypass) is the single best, guideline-concordant management. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (1) continuing protamine worsens the reaction; (2) fluid bolus + Trendelenburg loads a failing distended RV and ignores the pulmonary vasoconstriction; (3) heparin alone without stopping protamine/supporting circulation does not address the collapse (re-heparinization is only correct as part of returning to bypass); (4) misattributes the event to relaxant anaphylaxis and leaves the trigger running. Explanation is accurate and current: protamine ~1 mg per 100 units heparin, three reaction mechanisms correctly described, and risk factors (NPH/protamine-zinc insulin, prior exposure, fish allergy, vasectomy) are all correct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547753/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Protamine' article (Applefield & Krishnan), which explicitly covers heparin reversal, the catastrophic thromboxane-A2-mediated pulmonary hypertension/RV reaction, calcium/NO-mediated rapid-administration hypotension, anaphylaxis, and the exact risk-factor list. Citation is accurate and on-topic. Both checks pass.

stem: A 4-year-old, 16-kg child is brought to the GI suite for esophagogastroduodenoscopy. The gastroenterologist sprays a benzocaine-containing topical anesthetic on the oropharynx before the scope is passed. About 20 minutes later, the child appears dusky and cyanotic. The pulse oximeter reads 85% and does not improve with 100% oxygen by face mask. An arterial sample drawn at the same time shows a PaO2 of 250 mmHg, and the blood appears chocolate-brown. The child is hemodynamically stable and has no known enzyme deficiencies. Which is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Increase the FiO2 further and add PEEP, since the low saturation indicates a shunt physiology
choices.rationale: Supplemental oxygen does not correct methemoglobinemia because the problem is altered hemoglobin, not alveolar oxygenation; the high PaO2 already excludes a shunt as the cause.

choices.text: Administer intravenous methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg over several minutes
choices.rationale: Methylene blue acts as an electron donor that reduces methemoglobin back to functional hemoglobin and is the first-line treatment for symptomatic methemoglobinemia in a patient without G6PD deficiency.

choices.text: Administer intravenous 20% lipid emulsion as for local anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: Lipid emulsion is the antidote for systemic toxicity from amide local anesthetics, not for the oxidized-hemoglobin state produced by benzocaine, so it would not correct the cyanosis.

choices.text: Give intravenous ascorbic acid (vitamin C) as the definitive first-line antidote
choices.rationale: Vitamin C is reserved for patients who cannot receive methylene blue (e.g., G6PD deficiency); it is slower and not first-line in a child with normal enzyme status.

choices.text: Transfuse packed red blood cells emergently to raise the oxygen-carrying capacity
choices.rationale: Transfusion is not the initial treatment and does not reverse the methemoglobin already present; the specific antidote should be given first.

explanation: Benzocaine (and prilocaine in large doses) can oxidize hemoglobin iron to the ferric state, producing methemoglobin that cannot bind oxygen. Methemoglobin absorbs red and infrared light nearly equally, driving the pulse oximeter toward a characteristic plateau near 85% that does not rise with supplemental oxygen, while the PaO2 stays high and the blood appears chocolate-brown; co-oximetry confirms the diagnosis. First-line treatment in a patient without G6PD deficiency is IV methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg, reserving ascorbic acid for those in whom methylene blue is contraindicated.
subtopic: Local anesthetic adverse reactions: methemoglobinemia

verdict.reason: Textbook acquired methemoglobinemia: benzocaine spray, SpO2 plateau ~85% unresponsive to 100% O2, high PaO2 (250 mmHg) excluding shunt, chocolate-brown blood, no enzyme deficiency. Keyed answer (IV methylene blue 1-2 mg/kg) is the single best, first-line treatment and is correct. All distractors are genuinely inferior: O2/PEEP does not correct altered hemoglobin (high PaO2 excludes shunt); lipid emulsion treats LAST not oxidized-hemoglobin state; ascorbic acid is reserved for when methylene blue is contraindicated (e.g., G6PD deficiency); transfusion is not initial therapy and does not reverse existing MetHb. The main explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia methemoglobinemia keyword page resolves and is directly on-topic — it covers the Fe2+ to Fe3+ oxidation, benzocaine/prilocaine etiology, the 85% pulse-ox plateau (equal red/IR absorption), chocolate-brown blood, and methylene blue 1-2 mg/kg IV with vitamin C for G6PD deficiency, matching the question precisely. Minor nitpick (does not affect the key or answer choice): the lipid-emulsion distractor rationale describes benzocaine toxicity as from 'amide local anesthetics,' but benzocaine is an ester; this imprecision sits in a distractor rationale only and does not create ambiguity. Both clinical and reference are sound; keep.

stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and a baseline resting heart rate of 58 bpm is admitted to the ICU after a major abdominal operation. To provide cooperative sedation without respiratory depression, the team orders dexmedetomidine. The bedside nurse, eager to reach a target sedation level quickly, administers the full loading dose as a rapid intravenous bolus over about one minute rather than the recommended slower infusion. Within the first minute or two, which hemodynamic pattern is most likely to be observed?

choices.text: An immediate, isolated drop in blood pressure with reflex tachycardia
choices.rationale: Dexmedetomidine does not produce reflex tachycardia; vagal/central effects instead lower heart rate, and a bolus first raises rather than lowers pressure.

choices.text: A transient rise in blood pressure with bradycardia, followed by hypotension
choices.rationale: A rapid bolus produces a high peak plasma level that stimulates peripheral alpha-2B (and alpha-1) receptors causing transient vasoconstriction/hypertension with reflex and vagal bradycardia, then central alpha-2A activation lowers sympathetic outflow producing hypotension—the classic biphasic response.

choices.text: Sustained hypertension and tachycardia from catecholamine release
choices.rationale: Dexmedetomidine is a sympatholytic alpha-2 agonist that ultimately reduces, not increases, sympathetic outflow, so persistent hypertension with tachycardia is not expected.

choices.text: No meaningful change in heart rate or blood pressure regardless of infusion speed
choices.rationale: Rapid bolus administration is specifically associated with pronounced hemodynamic swings, so a flat response is incorrect.

choices.text: Profound hypotension and tachycardia that respond promptly to a fluid bolus alone
choices.rationale: Dexmedetomidine-induced bradycardia and hypotension after a rapid bolus can be refractory to fluids and vasopressors, and the heart rate falls rather than rises.

explanation: Dexmedetomidine is a highly selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist. When given as a rapid bolus, the high peak plasma concentration transiently activates peripheral postsynaptic alpha-2B (and some alpha-1) receptors, causing vasoconstriction with a brief rise in blood pressure and reflex/vagally mediated bradycardia; the drug then exerts its dominant central alpha-2A sympatholytic effect, reducing sympathetic outflow and producing hypotension and continued bradycardia. This biphasic pattern is why loading doses are infused slowly (typically over about 10 minutes), especially in older patients with low baseline heart rates.
subtopic: Alpha-2 agonist pharmacology: dexmedetomidine hemodynamics

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 1, "transient rise in BP with bradycardia, followed by hypotension") is the single best answer and reflects the classic, well-documented biphasic hemodynamic response to a rapid dexmedetomidine bolus. Mechanism is accurate and current: high peak plasma concentration activates peripheral postsynaptic alpha-2B (and alpha-1) receptors on vascular smooth muscle causing transient vasoconstriction/hypertension with baroreflex- and vagally-mediated bradycardia, then dominant central alpha-2A sympatholysis lowers sympathetic outflow producing hypotension with continued bradycardia. This is consistent with Ebert et al. and the FDA label (loading dose ~1 mcg/kg over 10 minutes). All four distractors are genuinely inferior: dexmedetomidine causes bradycardia not tachycardia (rules out 0 and 4), it is sympatholytic not catecholamine-releasing (rules out 2), and rapid bolus produces pronounced swings rather than a flat response (rules out 3). The stem's low baseline HR (58 bpm) in an elderly patient makes the bradycardia caveat clinically apt. Answer is unambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513303/ resolves to the StatPearls Dexmedetomidine chapter, directly on-topic. It confirms key claims (1600:1 alpha-2 selectivity; hypertension avoidable by slow administration or omitting the loading dose; adverse effects of hypotension, bradycardia, hypertension). The page is less granular on alpha-2A/2B subtypes, but it supports the stem and does not contradict any rationale. Both clinical and reference dimensions are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old man with a T2 glottic carcinoma is undergoing CO2 laser excision of a vocal cord lesion under general anesthesia with a laser-resistant endotracheal tube. To improve the surgical field the surgeon asks for the patient to be lightened; oxygen saturation drifts and the anesthesia provider increases the FiO2 to 0.6. Moments after the surgeon re-activates the laser, a flash and flame are seen at the glottis with smoke in the surgical field. Which of the following is the most appropriate FIRST action?

choices.text: Stop the flow of all airway gases and disconnect the circuit from the endotracheal tube
choices.rationale: Correct: removing the oxidizer by stopping gas flow and disconnecting the circuit is the first step that starves the fire, and is performed simultaneously with removing the burning tube.

choices.text: Flood the surgical field with saline before touching the endotracheal tube
choices.rationale: Incorrect: saline irrigation is important but follows removal of the burning tube and cessation of gas flow, because leaving an oxygen-fed tube in place sustains the fire.

choices.text: Increase fresh gas flow with 100% oxygen to flush smoke and improve oxygenation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: adding oxygen feeds the fire and is the single most dangerous action, since oxygen is the oxidizer driving combustion.

choices.text: Activate the OR fire alarm and evacuate the room before addressing the airway
choices.rationale: Incorrect: evacuation is premature and abandons the patient with a burning airway, whereas the airway fire must be extinguished immediately at the source.

choices.text: Administer a bolus of dexamethasone and racemic epinephrine to treat airway edema
choices.rationale: Incorrect: pharmacologic treatment of thermal airway injury addresses a downstream consequence and does nothing to extinguish an active fire.

explanation: In an airway fire, the immediate priorities are performed together: stop the flow of airway gases (remove the oxidizer), disconnect the circuit, and remove the burning endotracheal tube, then pour saline into the airway to extinguish residual embers. Only after the fire is out is the patient re-ventilated (initially with room air or the lowest feasible FiO2), the airway re-secured, and the damage assessed with bronchoscopy. This case also illustrates the root cause: a high FiO2 in the surgical field is the oxidizer that makes laser airway fires possible, so the FiO2 should be kept as low as tolerated whenever the laser is in use.
subtopic: Airway fire during laser airway surgery — immediate management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (A: stop flow of all airway gases + disconnect circuit, with rationale noting simultaneous removal of the burning tube) matches the ASA Practice Advisory airway-fire algorithm, whose first simultaneous steps are: stop airway gases/remove the oxidizer, remove the ETT, then pour saline. All distractors are genuinely inferior: B inverts the order (saline before removing an O2-fed tube), C adds the oxidizer and is actively dangerous, D abandons the patient/evacuates prematurely, E treats downstream thermal injury rather than extinguishing the fire. No competing "remove the ETT" distractor exists, so there is no ambiguity. The explanation correctly describes simultaneous first steps and re-ventilation with room air/lowest feasible FiO2, and correctly identifies high field FiO2 (raised to 0.6 before laser re-activation) as the root-cause oxidizer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/airway_fire_mgmt/ resolves to the OpenAnesthesia "Surgical Fires" page (updated Feb 2023), which is genuinely on-topic — it covers laser airway fires, the fire triangle, laser-resistant ETTs, keeping FiO2 low, and surgical-fire management. Citation is valid and relevant.

stem: A 5-year-old, 20 kg boy returns to the emergency department 7 hours after an outpatient tonsillectomy, swallowing repeatedly and spitting up dark blood. He is pale and anxious. Heart rate is 148/min, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, capillary refill 3 seconds; the parents report he has not had anything to drink since vomiting a large amount of blood-stained material at home. The otolaryngologist requests an urgent return to the OR to control the bleeding. Which of the following is the most appropriate anesthetic plan?

choices.text: Establish IV access and give a 20 mL/kg balanced crystalloid bolus, then perform a rapid-sequence induction with a second working suction immediately available
choices.rationale: Correct: this patient is hypovolemic with a stomach full of swallowed blood, so volume resuscitation before induction plus an RSI with redundant suction addresses both the hemodynamic and aspiration risks.

choices.text: Proceed directly to an inhalational induction with sevoflurane to avoid the discomfort of placing an IV in an awake child
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a slow inhalational induction in a hypovolemic, full-stomach child invites aspiration and hemodynamic collapse, and IV access must precede induction.

choices.text: Perform an awake intubation without sedation or topicalization to preserve airway reflexes
choices.rationale: Incorrect: awake intubation without any topicalization is impractical and traumatic in a frightened young child and is not the standard approach for the bleeding tonsil.

choices.text: Delay the case 6 hours to ensure NPO fasting guidelines are met before induction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: active hemorrhage is a surgical emergency that cannot wait, and ongoing swallowing of blood means the stomach will not empty regardless of elapsed fasting time.

choices.text: Give a full induction dose of propofol and an opioid to blunt the airway response before laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a full-dose propofol/opioid induction in a volume-depleted child risks profound hypotension, so reduced induction-agent doses after resuscitation are preferred.

explanation: The bleeding tonsil patient has two defining hazards: hypovolemia/anemia from blood loss and a full stomach of swallowed blood, both of which must be addressed before induction. The plan is to obtain IV access, resuscitate with crystalloid (and blood as needed), then perform a rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure, a reduced dose of induction agent, and a fast-acting neuromuscular blocker, while keeping a second functioning suction within reach because blood and clot can obscure the laryngeal view. Anticipating a potentially difficult, blood-filled airway and being ready to invoke the difficult-airway algorithm are essential.
subtopic: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage — anesthetic management

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The stem is a classic secondary post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage in a 5-year-old with signs of hypovolemia (HR 148, BP 88/52, cap refill 3s, pallor) plus a full stomach of swallowed blood. The keyed answer (Choice 0: IV access + 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, then RSI with a second working suction) correctly addresses both defining hazards and is the single best answer. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: inhalational induction (Choice 1) invites aspiration/collapse; awake intubation without topicalization (Choice 2) is impractical/traumatic in a young child; delaying 6h for NPO (Choice 3) ignores that active hemorrhage is an emergency and ongoing blood-swallowing prevents gastric emptying; full-dose propofol/opioid (Choice 4) risks profound hypotension. Rationales and explanation (RSI with cricoid pressure, reduced induction-agent dose, fast-acting NMB, redundant suction, difficult-airway readiness) are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia URL resolves (not a 404, last updated Feb 2023) and is directly on-topic; it explicitly corroborates pre-induction crystalloid resuscitation (10-20 mL/kg), RSI with cricoid pressure and reduced induction doses, the full-stomach-of-swallowed-blood hazard, and two functioning suction units. Only nuance: the source frames boluses as 10-20 mL/kg for venous oozing, so 20 mL/kg is at the upper end, but it is a standard, defensible pediatric bolus within the cited range and creates no ambiguity. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 62-year-old woman with a large anterior mediastinal mass and severe limited mouth opening from prior head-and-neck radiation is scheduled for biopsy. The team elects an awake fiberoptic intubation and plans bilateral superior laryngeal nerve blocks plus a transtracheal (translaryngeal) injection of local anesthetic to supplement topicalization. Anesthetizing the area innervated by the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve will most reliably blunt the gag and cough response arising from which structures?

choices.text: The posterior third of the tongue and the oropharyngeal wall
choices.rationale: Incorrect: sensation to the posterior third of the tongue and oropharynx is supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve, not the superior laryngeal nerve.

choices.text: The laryngeal mucosa above the vocal cords, including the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds
choices.rationale: Correct: the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve provides sensory innervation to the supraglottic larynx down to the level of the vocal cords, including the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds.

choices.text: The tracheal and laryngeal mucosa below the vocal cords
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the mucosa below the cords is supplied by the recurrent laryngeal nerve, which is the target of the transtracheal injection rather than the superior laryngeal block.

choices.text: The anterior two-thirds of the tongue and the floor of the mouth
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the anterior two-thirds of the tongue carries general sensation via the lingual nerve (trigeminal V3), unrelated to laryngeal innervation.

choices.text: The nasal mucosa and nasopharynx
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the nasal cavity is innervated by branches of the trigeminal nerve (sphenopalatine and anterior ethmoidal), which is topicalized separately for a nasal approach.

explanation: Complete topicalization for awake intubation requires blocking three sensory territories: the glossopharyngeal nerve (posterior third of tongue and oropharynx, mediating the gag reflex), the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (supraglottic larynx from the base of the epiglottis to the vocal cords), and the recurrent laryngeal nerve (mucosa below the cords and the trachea, reached by a transtracheal injection). Matching each nerve to its territory lets the provider choose the correct combination of blocks so the patient tolerates the scope and tube without coughing or gagging. In an anticipated difficult airway like this radiated, mediastinal-mass patient, maintaining spontaneous ventilation with a well-topicalized awake technique is the safest approach.
subtopic: Airway nerve blocks for awake fiberoptic intubation — laryngeal sensory innervation

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (vagus) provides sensory innervation to the supraglottic larynx — the laryngeal mucosa above the vocal cords including the inferior epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds (keyed answer, index 1). All distractors are correctly attributed to other nerves and are genuinely inferior: posterior third of tongue/oropharynx = glossopharyngeal (CN IX); below-cord/tracheal mucosa = recurrent laryngeal nerve (the transtracheal injection target); anterior two-thirds of tongue/floor of mouth = lingual nerve (V3); nasal mucosa/nasopharynx = trigeminal (sphenopalatine/anterior ethmoidal). The three-territory topicalization framework (IX, iSLN, RLN) is standard, current teaching, and the stem's clinical framing (mediastinal mass + radiation-induced limited mouth opening → awake fiberoptic with maintained spontaneous ventilation) is appropriate. Rationales are accurate. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia 'Awake Fiberoptic Intubation' page resolves, loads full content, and is directly on-topic — it explicitly covers glossopharyngeal, superior laryngeal (above cords), and recurrent laryngeal (below cords) sensory innervation and the corresponding nerve blocks. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 47-year-old woman with episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis undergoes laparoscopic adrenalectomy for a 5-cm pheochromocytoma. She was managed preoperatively with phenoxybenzamine for 14 days followed by addition of metoprolol, with liberal salt and fluid intake. Intraoperatively, manipulation of the tumor produced systolic pressures of 230 mmHg, controlled with a nitroprusside infusion and phentolamine boluses. Within two minutes of the surgeon ligating the adrenal vein and removing the gland, the arterial line shows a blood pressure of 68/40 mmHg with a heart rate of 110/min. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.text: Aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation and a phenylephrine or norepinephrine infusion as needed
choices.rationale: After venous ligation, the catecholamine source is abruptly removed while residual alpha-blockade and a vasodilated, relatively hypovolemic vascular bed persist, so volume plus vasopressor support is the correct response.

choices.text: Administer an additional bolus of phentolamine to relax the peripheral vasculature
choices.rationale: Phentolamine is an alpha-antagonist that would worsen the post-resection vasodilation and deepen the hypotension.

choices.text: Start an esmolol infusion to control the tachycardia
choices.rationale: Beta-blockade in this hypotensive, tachycardic-but-underfilled patient removes compensatory output and can precipitate cardiovascular collapse.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia with a propofol bolus to treat presumed light anesthesia
choices.rationale: A propofol bolus causes further vasodilation and myocardial depression, aggravating the hypotension rather than addressing its cause.

choices.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone for presumed adrenal insufficiency
choices.rationale: Acute adrenal insufficiency is not expected after unilateral adrenalectomy with a normal contralateral gland, and steroids would not address the immediate hemodynamic problem.

explanation: Once the adrenal (or tumor) vein is ligated, circulating catecholamines fall precipitously, unmasking the residual alpha-blockade, anesthetic-induced vasodilation, and the relative hypovolemia these patients carry; profound hypotension is the classic post-resection event. Management is volume expansion and vasopressor support (phenylephrine or norepinephrine), not further alpha- or beta-blockade. Persistent post-resection hypotension also warrants a glucose check, because loss of catecholamine-driven insulin suppression can produce rebound hypoglycemia.
subtopic: Pheochromocytoma — post-resection hemodynamic collapse
reference_title: Perioperative Management of Pheochromocytoma (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (IV fluids + phenylephrine/norepinephrine) is the single best management of post-resection hypotension after adrenal vein ligation. After the catecholamine source is removed, residual long-acting/noncompetitive alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine), anesthetic vasodilation, and relative hypovolemia cause profound hypotension treated with volume and vasopressors. All distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly rationalized: phentolamine (more alpha-antagonism worsens hypotension); esmolol (beta-blockade removes compensatory output in an underfilled patient, and the tachycardia here is appropriate compensation, not the target); propofol (adds vasodilation/myocardial depression); hydrocortisone (unilateral adrenalectomy with a normal contralateral gland does not cause acute adrenal insufficiency). The explanation's rebound-hypoglycemia point (loss of catecholamine-mediated insulin suppression) is accurate and current. Stem details (14 days phenoxybenzamine, beta-blocker added after alpha-blockade, liberal salt/fluid) are all correct preoperative practice. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK589634/ resolves; title matches exactly ('Perioperative Management of Pheochromocytoma', StatPearls/NCBI Bookshelf) and the page directly supports the keyed management ('After the ligation of the blood vessel of the tumor, a precipitous drop in blood pressure may occur... Fluid bolus with vasoconstrictors like phenylephrine and noradrenaline') and the rebound-hypoglycemia teaching point. No clinical or reference defects found.

stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a BMI of 38 presents for an elective inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia. He takes metformin and once-weekly semaglutide, with his most recent semaglutide dose three days ago. He reports occasional early satiety and bloating but no current nausea, and he has followed standard fasting instructions (no solids for 8 hours, clear liquids stopped 2 hours ago). According to the 2024 multisociety perioperative guidance on GLP-1 receptor agonists, which of the following is the most appropriate approach?

choices.text: Cancel and reschedule the case; weekly GLP-1 agonists must be held for at least three weeks before any anesthetic
choices.rationale: No guideline mandates a three-week hold, and routine cancellation is not recommended when the patient is otherwise prepared.

choices.text: Proceed, but assess aspiration risk and consider point-of-care gastric ultrasound and/or a rapid-sequence induction given symptoms suggesting delayed gastric emptying
choices.rationale: The multisociety guidance favors continuing therapy with individualized risk assessment, using gastric ultrasound and RSI when symptoms suggest retained contents, making this the most appropriate plan.

choices.text: Proceed routinely with a standard induction; GLP-1 agonists have no bearing on aspiration risk
choices.rationale: GLP-1 agonists delay gastric emptying and can leave residual gastric contents despite fasting, so ignoring aspiration risk is unsafe.

choices.text: Administer metoclopramide and proceed immediately without further airway risk assessment
choices.rationale: A prokinetic may help but does not replace formal aspiration-risk assessment and an appropriate airway plan.

choices.text: Require the patient to have held the drug for a full month and to be on insulin instead before surgery
choices.rationale: Switching to insulin and a one-month hold are not part of the guidance and would needlessly delay care.

explanation: The 2024 multisociety guidance endorsed/affirmed by the ASA moved away from blanket discontinuation and toward continuing GLP-1 receptor agonists with individualized, shared decision-making. When symptoms suggest delayed gastric emptying (early satiety, bloating, nausea), reasonable strategies include a preoperative clear-liquid diet, point-of-care gastric ultrasound to assess residual contents, and rapid-sequence induction to mitigate aspiration risk. Routine cancellation or rigid multi-week holds are not supported.
subtopic: Perioperative GLP-1 receptor agonist management and aspiration risk
reference_title: Multi-society Clinical Practice Guidance for the Safe Use of Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists in the Perioperative Period (PMC)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (B) is the single best answer and aligns precisely with the 2024 multisociety GLP-1RA perioperative guidance. The patient is on once-weekly semaglutide (last dose 3 days ago, at peak effect within the dosing interval) with GI symptoms suggesting delayed gastric emptying (early satiety, bloating). The guidance favors continuation with individualized aspiration-risk assessment, point-of-care gastric ultrasound to assess retained contents, and RSI vs. cancellation when concern exists — exactly choice B. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: A (3-week hold) and E (1-month hold + insulin switch) cite holds that do not exist in the guidance (which suggests at most a 1-week hold for weekly agents and discourages routine cancellation); C ignores the well-established delayed-gastric-emptying/aspiration risk; D substitutes a prokinetic for formal airway risk assessment. Rationales are all accurate. The explanation's mention of nausea as a delayed-emptying symptom does not conflict with the stem (early satiety and bloating independently justify the concern). No ambiguity or wrong key. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11666732/ resolves to the exact PMC-hosted 2024 'Multi-society clinical practice guidance for the safe use of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists in the perioperative period,' authored by AGA, ASMBS, ASA, ISPCOP, and SAGES. The page confirms shared decision-making over blanket discontinuation, POCUS for retained contents, RSI vs. cancellation language, and a 1-week (not multi-week) hold for weekly formulations — fully on-topic and supports every claim in the explanation.

stem: A 34-year-old woman with poorly controlled Graves disease presents for emergency appendectomy. She did not take her methimazole this week. Ninety minutes into general anesthesia, she becomes febrile to 40.1 C, heart rate 165/min in atrial fibrillation, blood pressure 90/50 mmHg, and end-tidal CO2 is rising despite increased minute ventilation. The soda lime canister is not unusually warm and there is no masseter rigidity. The team suspects intraoperative thyroid storm. Which of the following best reflects correct management priorities?

choices.text: Give a beta-blocker (e.g., esmolol or propranolol) first, then a thionamide such as propylthiouracil, and add iodine only after the thionamide has been started
choices.rationale: This sequence controls adrenergic effects and inhibits new hormone synthesis before iodine is given, and giving iodine after the thionamide avoids substrate-driven hormone production—exactly the recommended order.

choices.text: Administer dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg immediately, as this presentation is diagnostic of malignant hyperthermia
choices.rationale: Absence of masseter/generalized rigidity, the Graves history, and atrial fibrillation point to thyroid storm rather than MH, so dantrolene is not the primary therapy.

choices.text: Give iodine (e.g., potassium iodide) first to rapidly block hormone release, then start a thionamide several hours later
choices.rationale: Giving iodine before a thionamide can supply substrate for new hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow effect) and worsen the storm, so the order is wrong.

choices.text: Withhold beta-blockade because of the hypotension and treat with intravenous fluids and acetaminophen alone
choices.rationale: The hypotension here is largely rate-related from the tachyarrhythmia, and rate control with a titratable beta-blocker is a cornerstone of therapy rather than something to withhold.

choices.text: Treat the fever with aspirin and apply warming measures to avoid shivering
choices.rationale: Aspirin displaces thyroid hormone from binding proteins and raises free hormone, and active warming is exactly wrong for hyperthermia—cooling is indicated.

explanation: Intraoperative thyroid storm can mimic malignant hyperthermia (fever, tachycardia, rising CO2), but the Graves history, missed antithyroid medication, and atrial fibrillation favor storm; the absence of rigidity and a normally warm circuit argue against MH. Treatment targets multiple steps: beta-blockade for adrenergic control and rate, a thionamide (PTU or methimazole) to block synthesis, iodine after the thionamide to block release, corticosteroids, and active cooling. Aspirin should be avoided because it increases free thyroid hormone.
subtopic: Intraoperative thyroid storm — recognition and treatment sequence

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 0) is the single best answer and matches standard thyroid-storm management. Correct sequence: beta-blockade for adrenergic/rate control, then a thionamide (PTU or methimazole) to block new hormone synthesis, then iodine roughly an hour AFTER the thionamide to block release without supplying substrate, plus corticosteroids and active cooling. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (1) dantrolene/MH is correctly excluded by the Graves history, AFib, absence of masseter rigidity, and a normally-warm soda lime canister; (2) iodine-before-thionamide is correctly wrong because it can drive synthesis; (3) withholding beta-blockade is wrong since the hypotension is largely rate-related and titratable beta-blockade (esmolol) is a cornerstone; (4) aspirin raises free thyroid hormone by displacing it from binding proteins and warming is the opposite of indicated cooling. The stem honestly flags the storm-vs-MH mimicry but provides adequate discriminators, so there is no genuine ambiguity. Minor imprecision: distractor 2's rationale labels the substrate-driven worsening as the 'Jod-Basedow effect,' which is a loose use of that eponym (Jod-Basedow classically describes iodine-induced hyperthyroidism in an autonomous/nodular gland); however this appears only in a wrong-answer rationale and does not affect the key, the correct answer's rationale, or the main explanation. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448095/ resolves to StatPearls 'Thyroid Storm,' which is directly on-topic and explicitly confirms the key teaching points, including 'Always administer thionamide before starting iodine solution (SSKI) therapy,' the ~1-hour interval, and that 'Aspirin should be avoided.' Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a remote drug-eluting coronary stent (placed 5 years ago, asymptomatic, on aspirin and atorvastatin) presents to the operating room 9 days after pars plana vitrectomy and intravitreal injection of perfluoropropane (C3F8) gas for a retinal detachment in the left eye. He now requires general anesthesia for an urgent open reduction of an ankle fracture. As the certified anesthesiologist assistant building the anesthetic plan with the attending, which intraoperative decision is most critical to prevent catastrophic ophthalmic injury?

choices.text: Avoid nitrous oxide entirely and use an air/oxygen or oxygen/sevoflurane mixture for maintenance
choices.rationale: Correct: nitrous oxide diffuses into the closed intraocular gas bubble far faster than the gas (or nitrogen) can leave, expanding the non-compliant globe, spiking intraocular pressure, and risking central retinal artery occlusion; C3F8 persists for weeks so N2O must be avoided until the bubble fully resorbs.

choices.text: Position the patient strictly supine to keep the gas bubble away from the macula
choices.rationale: Incorrect: postoperative head positioning relates to surgical tamponade goals, not to the intraoperative anesthetic hazard, and supine positioning does nothing to prevent N2O-driven bubble expansion.

choices.text: Maintain mean arterial pressure above 90 mmHg to ensure retinal perfusion
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while perfusion matters, simply raising blood pressure will not protect the retina if intraocular pressure rises high enough to occlude the central retinal artery, and it ignores the actual mechanism of harm.

choices.text: Limit the total anesthetic time to less than 100 minutes regardless of agents used
choices.rationale: Incorrect: although prolonged ischemia worsens outcome, restricting case length does not address the root cause and is not a substitute for simply avoiding the offending agent.

choices.text: Use a 70% nitrous oxide/30% oxygen mixture but discontinue it 15 minutes before emergence
choices.rationale: Incorrect: even brief N2O exposure with an existing intraocular gas bubble can rapidly expand it; the 15-minute rule applies to stopping N2O before a surgeon injects gas, not to a patient who already has a bubble in place.

explanation: Nitrous oxide is roughly 34 times more soluble than nitrogen, so it enters a closed intraocular gas bubble much faster than the bubble's gas can diffuse out. In the non-compliant eye this raises intraocular pressure abruptly and can cause central retinal artery occlusion and permanent vision loss. SF6 bubbles persist about 7-10 days and C3F8 bubbles for up to 4-6 weeks, so N2O must be avoided for the entire lifetime of the bubble. The remote stent makes this patient ASA III but does not change the central ophthalmic safety rule.
subtopic: Nitrous oxide and intraocular gas tamponade in vitreoretinal surgery

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. The keyed answer (avoid N2O) is the single best answer: a patient 9 days after C3F8 intravitreal gas injection still has a long-lived gas bubble (C3F8 persists ~4-6 weeks), and N2O—being ~30-34x more soluble than nitrogen—diffuses in faster than the bubble gas leaves, expanding the non-compliant globe, spiking IOP, and risking CRAO/permanent vision loss. This is a classic high-yield board fact. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: supine positioning (B) is a postoperative surgical-tamponade concern, not the intraoperative anesthetic hazard; raising MAP (C) and limiting case time (D) do not address the mechanism; and the strongest trap (E) correctly distinguishes the 'stop N2O 15 min before gas injection' rule (for a surgeon about to inject gas) from a patient who already has a bubble—any N2O is contraindicated. The remote DES/aspirin history is appropriate distractor noise that correctly does not alter the ophthalmic rule. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/nitrous-oxide-closed-spaces/ resolves, matches the cited title, and is on-topic—it states N2O is ~30x more soluble than nitrogen and explicitly lists 'retinal surgery creating an intraocular gas bubble' as a contraindication. Only nitpick: the explanation's '34 times' vs the page's '30 times' solubility figure is a well-known literature range (Miller cites ~34x), not an error and it does not affect the answer.

stem: A 19-year-old man is brought emergently for repair of a ruptured (open) globe after being struck in the right eye with a baseball bat 90 minutes ago. He ate a full meal shortly before the injury and is in pain and retching. Vitals are stable, airway exam is reassuring, and he has no personal or family history of anesthetic problems. The attending plans rapid sequence induction. Regarding the choice of neuromuscular blocker to secure the airway while protecting the open globe, which approach is best supported by current evidence?

choices.text: Give a high-dose (1.2 mg/kg) rocuronium for rapid onset, with sugammadex available as a rescue reversal agent
choices.rationale: Correct: high-dose rocuronium produces intubating conditions within about 60 seconds without the transient rise in intraocular pressure that depolarization causes, and the availability of sugammadex allows rapid reversal if intubation fails, making it the preferred relaxant for the full-stomach open globe.

choices.text: Give succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg because its rapid offset is the safest option in a patient with a full stomach
choices.rationale: Incorrect: succinylcholine transiently raises intraocular pressure (via tonic extraocular muscle contraction and other mechanisms) and risks extrusion of intraocular contents, so it is generally avoided when a non-depolarizer plus reversal is available.

choices.text: Avoid all neuromuscular blockers and intubate under deep volatile anesthesia alone to keep intraocular pressure low
choices.rationale: Incorrect: intubating without paralysis in a full-stomach patient invites coughing and bucking, which raise intraocular pressure far more than a relaxant would and increase aspiration risk.

choices.text: Use a standard intubating dose of cisatracurium and proceed once the train-of-four shows no twitches
choices.rationale: Incorrect: cisatracurium has a slow onset (3-5 minutes), which is unacceptable for rapid sequence induction in a patient at high aspiration risk.

choices.text: Pretreat with a defasciculating dose of rocuronium, then give succinylcholine 1 mg/kg
choices.rationale: Incorrect: defasciculation does not reliably prevent the succinylcholine-associated rise in intraocular pressure, and this approach needlessly retains the depolarizer's ocular risk.

explanation: The open globe with a full stomach forces a balance between aspiration risk (favoring rapid sequence induction) and the need to avoid sustained increases in intraocular pressure that could extrude ocular contents. Succinylcholine produces a transient rise in intraocular pressure and is traditionally avoided when alternatives exist. High-dose rocuronium (1.0-1.2 mg/kg) achieves intubating conditions comparable to succinylcholine within about 60 seconds without that ocular pressure spike, and the availability of sugammadex provides a rescue path if a can't-intubate situation arises.
subtopic: Open globe injury with full stomach: induction and neuromuscular blocker selection

verdict.reason:
REFERENCE (B): The reference_url (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499984/) resolves and matches the cited title exactly ("Succinylcholine Chloride - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf"). It is genuinely on-topic, covering succinylcholine RSI dosing (confirms 1.5 mg/kg, ~60s onset) and the succinylcholine-IOP relationship. Sound.

CLINICAL (A): The keyed answer (high-dose rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg with sugammadex available) is the contemporary, evidence-aligned best choice for a full-stomach open globe and is factually accurate (~60s intubating conditions, no depolarization-induced IOP spike, sugammadex rescue). The other distractors are genuinely inferior: no-NMB/deep-volatile invites coughing/bucking and aspiration (correctly wrong); cisatracurium is too slow for RSI (correctly wrong); defasciculation + succinylcholine does not reliably blunt the succinylcholine IOP rise (rationale accurate). The succinylcholine mechanism (tonic EOM contraction raising IOP) is correct.

Adversarial note: The strongest competing answer is succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg, since the largely-theoretical extrusion risk is debated and rapid offset is advantageous in a can't-intubate scenario; succinylcholine is still taught as acceptable for open globe with full stomach. However, the stem explicitly asks what is "best supported by current evidence" and highlights sugammadex availability, which steers unambiguously to the rocuronium answer as the single best choice. Choice B's rationale ("generally avoided when a non-depolarizer plus reversal is available") is defensible rather than an error. Not ambiguous enough to fail. Key is correct, rationales accurate, citation valid.

stem: A 71-year-old woman with chronic open-angle glaucoma is scheduled for an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. Her ophthalmic medication list includes echothiophate iodide (an anticholinesterase) eye drops, which she has used in both eyes twice daily for the past several months. The attending asks you, the certified anesthesiologist assistant, to anticipate the most relevant anesthetic implication of this medication. Which complication should most concern you?

choices.text: Prolonged neuromuscular blockade and apnea if succinylcholine is administered
choices.rationale: Correct: echothiophate is absorbed systemically through the nasolacrimal mucosa and irreversibly inhibits plasma (pseudo)cholinesterase, so the enzyme that normally metabolizes succinylcholine is markedly depleted and a standard dose can produce greatly prolonged paralysis and apnea.

choices.text: Resistance to non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers requiring markedly increased doses
choices.rationale: Incorrect: anticholinesterase eye drops do not induce resistance to non-depolarizers; their key systemic effect is reduced metabolism of ester-type and depolarizing agents, not altered aminosteroid requirements.

choices.text: Malignant hyperthermia triggered by volatile agents because of the drug's organophosphate structure
choices.rationale: Incorrect: echothiophate is unrelated to malignant hyperthermia, which is a pharmacogenetic disorder of skeletal-muscle calcium release triggered by volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine in susceptible patients.

choices.text: Refractory intraoperative hypotension from systemic muscarinic blockade
choices.rationale: Incorrect: as an anticholinesterase the drug increases acetylcholine and would tend toward muscarinic (cholinergic) effects such as bradycardia, not blockade-induced hypotension.

choices.text: Accelerated metabolism of amide local anesthetics increasing the risk of subtherapeutic block
choices.rationale: Incorrect: amide local anesthetics are metabolized hepatically and are unaffected by plasma cholinesterase, so echothiophate has no meaningful effect on them.

explanation: Echothiophate is a long-acting topical anticholinesterase used for glaucoma; despite ocular administration it is absorbed systemically and irreversibly inhibits plasma cholinesterase, often reducing activity to 5% or less of normal. Because plasma cholinesterase is the enzyme that hydrolyzes succinylcholine (and mivacurium and ester local anesthetics), a normal dose can produce markedly prolonged paralysis and postoperative apnea. The drug should be stopped roughly 4-6 weeks before elective surgery if succinylcholine is anticipated, since enzyme recovery takes weeks. This is a classic example of a topical ophthalmic agent producing a clinically important systemic anesthetic interaction.
subtopic: Systemic effects of ophthalmic drugs: anticholinesterase eye drops and plasma cholinesterase

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Keyed answer A is the single best answer and is textbook-correct. Topical echothiophate (a long-acting organophosphate anticholinesterase for glaucoma) is absorbed systemically via nasolacrimal mucosa and irreversibly inhibits plasma (pseudo)cholinesterase, markedly prolonging succinylcholine-induced paralysis/apnea. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: B (non-depolarizer resistance) is false — if anything mivacurium would be prolonged, not aminosteroids; C (MH) correctly noted as an unrelated RYR1 pharmacogenetic disorder; D (muscarinic-blockade hypotension) is internally contradictory since an anticholinesterase causes cholinergic/muscarinic excess (bradycardia), not blockade; E is correctly keyed wrong because amide LAs are hepatically metabolized — and the explanation accurately notes echothiophate prolongs ESTER LAs and mivacurium.
